# Case Study: A Variable Admittance Diffusion Policy {.section-divider}

## Learning *What to Do* and *How Compliant to Be*

A diffusion policy that outputs motion **and** admittance gains on a Doosan arm.

- The robot runs a **variable admittance controller** as its low-level interface.
- A **diffusion policy** predicts, at every step, a motion command **plus** the stiffness `K` and damping ratio `ζ` the controller should use.
- Compliance is **learned from demonstrations**, not hand-tuned per task.

System at a glance: `Doosan m0609` · `Bota wrist F/T` · `Inspire hand` · `Third-person camera (RealSense / Orbbec)`

::: {.notes}
The abstract pipeline now becomes a concrete system. Everything we built up so far — a diffusion policy over multimodal action trajectories, and the variable-admittance idea of learning how compliant to be — comes together here on a 6-DOF Doosan m0609 collaborative arm: a single policy that outputs both a desired motion and the compliance gains for the controller. The one-line thesis: instead of fixing the robot's stiffness by hand for each task, we let the policy decide how compliant to be, moment by moment, conditioned on what it sees and feels. The rest of the block walks through the controller, what the policy predicts, how it is deployed in real time, and what we found.

"6-DOF" means the arm can control six independent task-space degrees of freedom: three translations and three rotations of the tool frame, within kinematic limits. "Admittance gains" are the parameters that set how measured force becomes commanded motion. $K$ is stiffness: high $K$ means the virtual spring resists displacement strongly. $\zeta$ is damping ratio: it determines whether the virtual dynamics oscillate, settle quickly, or feel sluggish. "Learned from demonstrations" means the policy observes examples of both motion and compliance choices and then predicts those quantities at deployment time.
:::

<!-- TODO citation: add reference to the project / thesis once available. -->

---

## The System Stack

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- **Robot:** Doosan **m0609**, 6 DOF, velocity interface (`speedl`).
- **Force sensing:** **Bota** wrist F/T sensor; per-episode tare, frame-corrected into the base frame.
- **End-effector:** Inspire multi-finger hand (open/grasp).
- **Vision:** a single **third-person** camera — Intel RealSense *or* Orbbec Femto (interchangeable, no wrist camera).
:::

```text
 cameras + F/T + proprioception
       │  (observations)
       ▼
 Diffusion Policy          ~20 Hz   (slow)
       │  Δpose, K, ζ, hand
       ▼
 Variable Admittance Ctrl  ~100 Hz  (fast)
       │  velocity command (speedl)
       ▼
 Doosan m0609 + Inspire hand
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Bottom layer is a real Doosan m0609 driven through its velocity command interface, speedl. Contact force is measured by a Bota force/torque sensor at the wrist; we tare it at the start of every episode while the arm is in free space, and we rotate the wrench into the robot base frame before using it — the admittance law must reason about forces in a consistent frame. On top sits the two-rate control structure the whole case study turns on: a slow diffusion policy at ~20 Hz that decides intent, and a fast admittance controller at ~100 Hz that turns that intent into safe, contact-aware motion. This implements the slow–fast policy interface pattern introduced earlier.

A wrench is a six-dimensional force/torque vector: three force components and three torque components. The sensor measures that wrench in its own sensor frame. The robot controller needs forces expressed in a consistent frame, here the robot base frame, otherwise a force along sensor x could be interpreted as a different physical direction. "Tare" means subtracting the sensor bias at the beginning of the episode so that zero roughly corresponds to no external contact load.
:::

---

## The Compliant Controller {.smaller}

The "fast" loop: a virtual mass–spring–damper between measured force and commanded motion.

::: {.equation-card}
$$
M\,\ddot{\Delta x} + B\,\dot{\Delta x} + K\,(\Delta x - \Delta x_{\text{ref}}) = F_{\text{ext}}
$$
:::

- Damping is **derived**, not free: $B = 2\,\zeta\sqrt{M\,K}$ (per axis).
- Virtual mass fixed (`M_trans ≈ 5 kg`, `M_rot ≈ 0.5 kg·m²`); **`K` and `ζ` are the knobs**.
- Runs at **~100 Hz**: integrates force → reference velocity → `speedl`.
- Hard safety limits on velocity and displacement at every step.

::: {.notes}
The same mass-spring-damper admittance model from the generic part, now as the actual low-level controller. Two points. First, damping is not independent: we set it from stiffness and a damping ratio ζ via B = 2ζ√(MK), so the system stays well-damped as stiffness changes — the policy only reasons about K and ζ, not absolute damping. Second, the virtual mass is fixed; what shapes interaction behavior is K and ζ. Everything above this layer exists to choose those two numbers well. The loop runs near 100 Hz, integrates the measured external force into a reference velocity, and sends it as a velocity command, with hard caps on velocity and displacement so a bad command can never become a fast or large motion.

$M$ is the fixed virtual mass or inertia. $B$ is damping, called $D$ in the previous generic slides; the symbol changes but the physical meaning is the same. $K$ is stiffness. $\Delta x$ is the current displacement of the admittance state, and $\Delta x_{\text{ref}}$ is the policy's desired reference displacement or target offset. $\dot{\Delta x}$ and $\ddot{\Delta x}$ are velocity and acceleration of that admittance state. $F_{\text{ext}}$ is the measured external force. The controller numerically integrates this differential equation at the fast loop rate, then sends the resulting velocity through `speedl`. Per-axis means the scalar formula is applied separately to translational and rotational components, with different virtual mass values for translation and rotation.
:::

---

## The Policy Output: Motion *and* Compliance

The action is a **9-D** vector predicted over a horizon (receding horizon, re-planned):

![](assets/figures/dp_action_vector.svg){width=92%}

- **6** pose deltas → desired motion · **1** stiffness `K` + **1** damping ratio `ζ` → *how compliant to be* · **1** binary hand open/grasp.

*This is the "trajectory + compliance parameters" architecture, realized.*

::: {.notes}
The answer to the earlier question — "should the policy output low-level control parameters directly?" — is yes in this system. The action is nine numbers: six pose deltas defining the desired motion, then stiffness K and damping ratio ζ for the admittance controller, and a binary hand open/close. A single forward pass answers both questions at once — what the end-effector should do, and how stiff or soft to be while doing it. Like any diffusion policy, it predicts a short horizon of future actions and we re-plan as we go. The compliance channel is what makes this more than a motion policy: the network can stiffen to push a pipe into a hole and then go soft the instant a latch releases.

The six pose deltas are usually three translational components plus three rotational components, expressed as small increments rather than absolute poses. The stiffness output is a scalar or compact gain parameter used by the admittance controller; in a more general system it could be a vector of per-axis stiffnesses. The damping-ratio output $\zeta$ is dimensionless. The hand command is binary because the task interface only needs open versus grasp. Over a horizon, this means the diffusion model is not producing one 9-D vector but a sequence of 9-D vectors.
:::

---

## Conditioning: Vision + Force + State

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- **RGB image** (84×84) → ResNet-18 encoder + spatial-softmax keypoints.
- **Wrench** (6-D force/torque from the Bota sensor).
- **Proprioceptive state** (12-D: TCP pose + hand joints).
- Short **observation history** (last 2 frames) → predict next action chunk.
:::

![](assets/figures/dp_conditioning.svg){width=100%}
:::

- Force feedback lets the policy *react to contact*, not just to pixels.
- Modular variants exist: **vision-only**, **vision+force**, **vision+force+state**.

::: {.notes}
Conditioning follows the menu from earlier: vision, force/torque, proprioception. The image is downsized and passed through a small ResNet encoder with a spatial-softmax head that turns the feature map into a handful of keypoints. The wrench and the robot/hand state are concatenated as low-dimensional inputs. The policy looks at a short history — the last couple of observations — and predicts the next chunk of actions. Force is the interesting modality: because the policy can see contact forces, it can modulate both its motion and its commanded stiffness in response to what it touches. We built several variants — vision only, vision plus force, vision plus force plus state — precisely to ask which signals actually matter, the experiment a few slides from now.

ResNet-18 is a convolutional neural network with residual skip connections. A CNN uses learned local filters over the image; a residual connection lets a layer learn a correction to its input rather than a completely new representation, which helps train deeper networks. Spatial softmax converts a feature map into expected 2D keypoint locations: instead of keeping every pixel feature, it summarizes where strong activations are. This is useful for robotics because keypoint-like features are compact and more geometric than a raw feature map. The wrench is 6-D because it contains three forces and three torques. The 12-D proprioceptive state summarizes robot tool pose and hand joints. The two-frame history gives the policy a small amount of temporal information, enough to infer trends such as approaching contact or force increasing.
:::

---

## Slow Policy, Fast Compliance

![](assets/figures/dp_slow_fast_timeline.svg){width=90%}

- The policy sets **intent and compliance**; the controller handles **contact dynamics**.
- Fast inner loop = stability and safety even if the policy is occasionally noisy.
- Decouples a heavy generative model from a hard real-time control budget.

::: {.notes}
This architecture pays off in two ways. A diffusion policy is expensive to sample, so it cannot run inside a hard 100 Hz control loop — and we don't ask it to. The policy runs at about 20 Hz and only decides high-level intent and the compliance gains; the admittance controller runs roughly five times faster and is the thing that actually reacts to measured forces tick by tick. Two benefits. First, real-time feasibility: the slow expensive model and the fast hard-real-time loop are decoupled. Second, safety and stability: the inner mass-spring-damper loop is a physical low-pass filter on the policy's commands, so even a momentarily bad prediction gets absorbed rather than slammed into the robot. That is why pairing a learned policy with a compliant controller is more than a convenience — it is what makes deploying a generative policy on contact-rich hardware tractable.
:::

---

## Fitting Diffusion Into the Control Budget {.smaller}

Problem → levers → result:

- **Budget:** one policy query must fit a ~50 ms slot (20 Hz).
- **Trained** with full DDPM (~100 denoising steps) for quality.
- **Deployed** with **DDIM and far fewer steps (~10)** — fewer network evals.
- **Action chunking:** sample once, execute several steps, re-plan.
- **Systems fix:** a single-threaded executor removed GIL contention → predict time dropped from **~140 ms to ~11 ms**.
- **Result:** steady **~20 Hz** inference with large idle margin.

::: {.equation-card}
$$
T_{\text{query}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{enc}} + K\cdot T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{comm}}
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
This connects directly back to the inference-efficiency part of the lecture. The control budget is about 50 ms per query. We train with the full many-step DDPM sampler because it gives the best action quality offline, but we don't deploy it that way — at run time we switch to DDIM with roughly ten denoising steps, the classic latency-quality trade-off: far fewer network evaluations for a small quality cost we validate. Action chunking helps too: we sample a short horizon once and execute several steps before re-planning, amortizing inference cost. The biggest single win, though, was a plain systems bug, not a modeling change — the ROS node used a multi-threaded executor and the Python GIL was thrashing between the camera, force, and pose callbacks; switching to a single-threaded executor cut prediction time by more than an order of magnitude. The lesson: "make the diffusion policy real-time" is partly sampler choice and partly ordinary engineering.

$T_{\text{sense}}$ is time to receive camera, force, and state data. $T_{\text{enc}}$ is time to run the image encoder and build the conditioning vector. $K\cdot T_{\text{net}}$ is denoising cost: number of sampler steps times cost per neural-network evaluation. $T_{\text{comm}}$ is communication and middleware overhead. A 20 Hz loop gives 50 ms per cycle, but not all of that should be consumed by inference; some idle margin is needed for operating-system scheduling and sensor timing variation.
:::

---

## DDPM vs DDIM on the Real Robot {.smaller}

*Same policy, same fridge task, same hardware — only the **deployment sampler** changes.*

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex; gap:56px; justify-content:center; align-items:flex-start; margin-top:6px;">
  <div style="text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:#dc2626; margin-bottom:6px;">DDPM &middot; ~100 steps</div>
    <video data-autoplay loop muted playsinline controls style="height:560px; border-radius:10px; box-shadow:0 4px 14px rgba(0,0,0,.18);">
      <source src="assets/videos/ddpm_fridge.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    </video>
    <div style="color:#637083; margin-top:8px;">too slow for the loop &rarr; <strong>hesitant, stop-and-go</strong> &middot; ~40&nbsp;s</div>
  </div>
  <div style="text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-weight:700; color:#16a34a; margin-bottom:6px;">DDIM &middot; ~10 steps</div>
    <video data-autoplay loop muted playsinline controls style="height:560px; border-radius:10px; box-shadow:0 4px 14px rgba(0,0,0,.18);">
      <source src="assets/videos/ddim_fridge.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    </video>
    <div style="color:#637083; margin-top:8px;">holds ~20&nbsp;Hz &rarr; <strong>smooth, continuous</strong> &middot; ~16&nbsp;s</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.notes}
The latency-quality trade-off is visible on the real robot, not just in a table. Both clips run the exact same trained policy on the exact same fridge-opening task; the only thing that changes is the sampler used at deployment. On the left, the full DDPM sampler with around a hundred denoising steps: each policy query is far too slow for the ~50 ms control budget, so the robot stalls between action chunks and the motion is hesitant and stop-and-go — the whole task drags out. On the right, DDIM with about ten steps keeps the loop near 20 Hz, so the motion is smooth and continuous and the task finishes in well under half the time. The exact durations should be treated as approximate, because they are read from the clips, but the qualitative result is clear: fewer denoising steps is not an abstract speedup, it is the difference between a robot that stutters and one that flows.
:::

---

## Collecting Compliant Demonstrations

- Human teleoperates the arm through the **same admittance controller** used at deploy time.
- Each step logs: pose delta, **commanded K and ζ**, hand state, wrench, proprioception, RGB.
- Recorded at **~50 Hz**; **force sensor tared per episode**.
- **"Pre-image" targets:** we record the human's *intended* command + stiffness, **before** the controller transforms it — so the policy learns intent, not the post-controller velocity.
- Example scale: pipe-insertion set ≈ **200 episodes / ~100k frames**.

::: {.notes}
Demonstrations are collected by teleoperating the robot through the exact same compliant controller we deploy with, so there is no train/deploy mismatch in the control interface. At every timestep we log the full picture: the motion delta, the stiffness and damping the operator was using, the hand state, the measured wrench, the robot state, and the camera image, at about 50 Hz, with the force sensor re-zeroed at the start of each episode. One subtle but important choice is the training target. We record the "pre-image" — the operator's intended command and chosen stiffness before the admittance controller turns it into a velocity — rather than the post-controller output. That way the policy learns the human's intent and compliance strategy directly, and the admittance layer does the same job at deploy time as during teaching. A representative dataset is on the order of a couple hundred episodes and a hundred thousand frames.

The "pre-image" point is important enough to restate. The admittance controller transforms force plus intended motion into an actual velocity command. If we trained on the post-controller velocity, the policy would have to imitate a mixture of human intent and controller reaction. By training on the pre-controller command, the policy learns what the demonstrator wanted, while the same controller handles the physical force response during deployment. This keeps the learning target closer to decision-making rather than low-level dynamics.
:::

---

## Where Should Compliance Come From? {.smaller}

*Same task (open a fridge), same data — vary only how compliance is produced.*

| Variant | Policy outputs | Sees force? | Val. loss |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| Motion only + force obs | Δpose + hand | ✔ | **0.028** |
| Learned K+ζ, force-blind | Δpose + **K,ζ** + hand | ✗ | 0.030 |
| Motion only, external compliance | Δpose + hand | ✗ | 0.031 |
| Learned K+ζ, force-aware | Δpose + **K,ζ** + hand | ✔ | 0.031 |
| Raw velocity, **no admittance** | velocity + hand | ✗ | 0.042 |
| BC-RNN baseline (not comparable) | Δpose + K,ζ | ✔ | 0.29 |

::: {.warning}
**Validation loss ≠ task success** — these are offline fit metrics; on-robot evaluation is the real test.
:::

::: {.notes}
The research question becomes an actual ablation. We hold the task and dataset fixed — opening a fridge door — and vary only one thing: where compliance comes from. Does the policy predict the stiffness and damping itself, force-aware or force-blind? Or does it just predict motion and let an external controller handle compliance? Or do we drop the admittance layer entirely and predict raw velocity? Honest observations. First, the variant with no admittance layer is clearly worst — removing the compliant controller hurts, which supports the whole premise. Second, among the rest the numbers are close, and the motion-only policy that can see force fits the demonstrations best, slightly ahead of explicitly predicting K and ζ. That is genuinely interesting and slightly humbling: force observation may matter more than force-derived gain prediction, at least by this metric. And the crucial caveat — this is offline validation loss, how well the network reproduces the demonstrations, not task success on the robot. The BC-RNN row uses a different loss and is there only to show diffusion fits this multimodal data far better, not for a head-to-head number. On-robot evaluation is the next step and the real arbiter.

"Policy outputs" says what the network predicts as its action. "Sees force?" says whether the force/torque signal is included in the observation conditioning. "Val. loss" is validation loss on held-out demonstration windows, not physical success rate. "Force-blind" means the policy predicts gains without observing contact force. "External compliance" means compliance is supplied by a controller or fixed mechanism rather than learned as part of the action. "Raw velocity, no admittance" removes the mass-spring-damper safety layer and asks the policy to directly command velocity, which is a much more brittle interface for contact.
:::

<!-- TODO citation: ablation numbers from project experiments. -->

---

## Guardrails on Learned Gains

- Predicted gains are **clipped to an admissible envelope**: e.g. `K ∈ [50, 5000] N/m`, `ζ ∈ [0.3, 5.0]`.
- **Force-safety saturation:** as contact force approaches the limit, `K` is ramped toward 0 → the arm yields instead of pushing harder.
- **Impact / release latch:** on a sudden force drop (a latch or clip letting go), briefly force low `K` + high damping to absorb the transient.
- Hard caps on commanded velocity and displacement at the controller.

::: {.equation-card}
$$
K_{min} \preceq K_d \preceq K_{max},
\qquad
D_{min} \preceq D_d \preceq D_{max}
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Learned gains have to be projected into a safe envelope, and this is what that looks like in practice. The policy's stiffness and damping are clipped to fixed bounds before they ever reach the controller — the network simply cannot command values outside the admissible range. On top of the static clip there are two dynamic behaviors. One is force-safety saturation: as the measured contact force climbs toward the limit, we ramp stiffness down toward zero, so the robot yields rather than fighting an obstacle — passive protection against jamming. The other is an impact, or release, latch: when a building force suddenly collapses — exactly what happens the instant a fridge latch or clip lets go — we briefly drop to very low stiffness and high damping to absorb the snap. And underneath it all the controller still hard-caps velocity and displacement. This is the concrete answer to "can learned compliance be made safe" — not certifiably yet, but bounded, monitored, and backed by a controller that fails soft.

In the inequality, $K_{min}$ and $K_{max}$ are lower and upper stiffness limits. $D_{min}$ and $D_{max}$ are lower and upper damping limits, even though the implementation may command damping indirectly through $\zeta$. The symbol $\preceq$ means each commanded gain must lie inside the allowed interval or matrix cone. Clipping is a projection: if the network outputs an unsafe value, the controller replaces it with the nearest admissible value. This does not prove formal safety, but it prevents obvious out-of-range commands from reaching hardware.
:::

---

## Case Study — Takeaways

- A diffusion policy can output **motion + compliance (K, ζ)** in one shot, conditioned on vision + force.
- A **slow policy / fast admittance** split makes a heavy generative model deployable on real contact-rich hardware.
- Real-time feasibility is **sampler choice + engineering** (DDIM, chunking, executor fix → ~20 Hz).
- Compliance can be **learned from demonstrations** instead of hand-tuned; safety comes from **bounded gains + a fail-soft controller**.

::: {.takeaway}
Open: offline fit ≠ task success — **on-robot evaluation**, and whether explicit learned gains beat force-aware motion-only, remain to be settled.
:::

::: {.notes}
We end with an end-to-end system where a diffusion policy decides both what the robot should do and how compliant it should be, from vision and force, and a fast admittance controller turns that into safe physical interaction. The slow-fast split is the key architectural idea that makes a generative policy runnable on a real arm, and getting it real-time was as much careful engineering as modeling. We can learn compliance from demonstrations rather than tuning it by hand, and we keep it safe with bounded gains and a controller that yields under load. The honest open questions remain: our comparisons so far are offline, and the most interesting finding — that simply observing force might matter more than explicitly predicting stiffness — needs to be tested where it counts, on the robot, against actual task success. That is exactly the research direction this work opens up.
:::
